# Core Doctrine

---

## Introduction

Doctrine defines how power is exercised.

If the Charter establishes purpose, Doctrine establishes behavior.

Aegis doctrine rests on a structural premise:

Intelligence must be governed before it is permitted to act.

This doctrine is not aesthetic.  
It is operational.

---

## Article I — Constraint Before Capability

Constraint is not the limitation of intelligence.  
It is the condition under which intelligence becomes trustworthy.

In systems engineering, constraint prevents undefined behavior.  
In security engineering, least privilege prevents abuse of access.[^1]  
In constitutional governance, separation of powers prevents concentration of authority.[^2]

Aegis extends these principles to artificial intelligence.

Before execution:

- Scope must be declared.
- Interfaces must be enumerated.
- Tools must be bounded.
- External surfaces must be identified.
- Failure conditions must be explicit.

If constraint cannot be expressed, execution is denied.

---

## Article II — Governance Before Execution

Execution is subordinate to governance.

Governance defines:

- Permitted modes of operation.
- Threat posture.
- Authority requirements.
- Escalation pathways.
- Audit expectations.

Execution without governance is equivalent to actuation without control feedback.[^3]

No action may precede classification.

---

## Article III — Transparency Before Trust

Trust is structural, not emotional.

A system earns trust when its behavior is legible.

Legibility requires:

- Clear input declaration.
- Explicit constraint.
- Visible authority context.
- Recorded threat level.
- Durable artifact production.

Opaque systems require blind trust.  
Blind trust does not scale.

---

## Article IV — Oversight Before Autonomy

Autonomy increases impact.  
Impact increases risk.  
Risk requires oversight.

Autonomy is therefore treated as a risk multiplier.

Escalation to higher threat levels requires:

- Explicit justification.
- Oversight approval.
- Defined rollback strategy.
- Durable logging.

At the highest threat level, execution requires detachment — physical or network isolation.[^4]

---

## Article V — Deny by Default

In the presence of ambiguity, Aegis defaults to denial.

Where:

- Threat posture is unclear,
- Scope is missing,
- Authority is unverifiable,
- Audit cannot be written,

Execution does not proceed.

This mirrors fail-safe engineering in safety-critical domains.[^5]

---

## Operational Maxim

If a system can act, it can harm.  
If it can harm, it must be governed.  
If it is governed, it may become worthy of trust.

[^1]: See [R03](references.html#r03-saltzer-schroeder-least-privilege-and-protection-principles).
[^2]: See [R04](references.html#r04-madison-federalist-no-51-separation-of-powers-checks-and-balances).
[^3]: See [R05](references.html#r05-ogata-modern-control-engineering-control-feedback-concepts).
[^4]: See [R06](references.html#r06-perrow-normal-accidents-high-risk-technologies).
[^5]: See [R07](references.html#r07-leveson-engineering-a-safer-world-systems-thinking-safety).
